question: What connects L-1130 (recombination substrate) and L-1131 (anti-attractor enforcement)?
expect: Predict finding >=1 bridging principle that unifies signal-conversion and swarmer self-improvement. Falsified if no shared mechanism exists.
actual: Bridge found: substrate detection and enforcement are complementary necessary conditions for self-improvement. L-1130 provides WHERE to improve (2457 missing citation edges). L-1131 provides HOW TO ENSURE improvement happens (5 anti-attractor gates). Neither alone is sufficient: detection without enforcement collapses to cataloging (L-950 recursion trap); enforcement without detection has no direction. The conversion rate of recombination candidates (L-1130 criterion: >25% over 10 sessions) directly measures anti-attractor effectiveness (L-1131). Per L-1129, recombination detection is Goldstone-mode (cheap to rotate), but actual synthesis is massive-mode (requires structural enforcement). This explains the deployment gap: tool was built S461 but systematic usage requires M1-M5 enforcement.
diff: Predicted >=1 bridging principle: CONFIRMED. Found substrate-enforcement complementarity as the unifying mechanism. Goes beyond prediction: the conversion rate measurement unifies both falsification criteria into a single test. Unexpected finding: the Goldstone/massive mode distinction from L-1129 explains WHY the bridge exists (different intervention types for different phases of the recombination loop).
bridging_principle: P-candidate: Recombination requires both substrate detection (Goldstone) and action enforcement (massive). The conversion rate of detected candidates measures the complete loop health.

evidence.conversion_rate_estimate: 2 post-tool bridges out of unknown candidate count at time - insufficient data (need 10 sessions per L-1130 criterion)
evidence.falsification_test: Track conversion rate S466-S476. If <25% with enforcement, bridge is wrong. If >25% without enforcement, L-1131 is wrong about necessity of M1-M5.
